Sideways translation field
Left and right panels are fixed; drag the slider to morph the middle panel.
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: a single large rectangular image plane (single interactive panel)
quiver/vector field of 2D arrows distributed on a uniform grid, showing the instantaneous motion (velocity) at each image point
dynamic annotation at the bottom center indicating current focal length: 'f=...' (updates as slider changes)
border rectangle framing the image plane
axis ticks/labels along image plane edges for reference

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Shows how varying focal length transforms the motion flow pattern: smaller f creates a divergent, perspective-like motion field; large f produces nearly uniform lateral motion vectors.

interactions.label: Show three f values side-by-side
interactions.teaches: Displays all three canonical f cases ('f=1/3','f=1','f=3') next to each other for direct visual comparison of the changing motion field structure.

interactions.label: Normalize arrow lengths
interactions.teaches: Scales all arrows to equal length so only their direction is visible, highlighting the difference in flow direction patterns without confusing length scaling.

demo_steps.title: Small f: highly divergent field
demo_steps.narration: Set focal length to f = 1/3. Notice how the arrows on the image plane fan outward from the center and change both direction and length rapidly across the panel. This pattern resembles the effect of a homography: points near the top and bottom move diagonally, and the velocity varies strongly with position. The magnitude and direction differences are greatest here.

demo_steps.title: Medium f: pattern begins to flatten
demo_steps.narration: Increase focal length to f = 1. The arrows now show less divergence—the central rows all point mostly to the right, with small vertical or diagonal variation near the image top and bottom. The flow still feels somewhat perspective-driven, but the effect is less pronounced. The overall magnitude of arrows increases in the central zone.

demo_steps.title: Large f: nearly uniform horizontal flow
demo_steps.narration: Push focal length up to f = 3. Now, all arrows across the entire image are almost perfectly horizontal and of similar length. This is the regime where $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and $\dot{y} \approx 0$: the entire scene appears to move as if under pure lateral translation, even though the underlying motion is actually rotation about the Y axis.

demo_steps.title: Direct comparison: three canonical f values
demo_steps.narration: Enable the comparison view to see all three focal lengths—f = 1/3, 1, and 3—lined up side-by-side. You can now directly compare how the motion field shape morphs from very divergent (left) to nearly uniform (right), making it clear how sensitive image motion flow is to the camera’s focal length.

demo_steps.title: Only direction: normalized vectors
demo_steps.narration: Now switch on arrow normalization. All arrows have the same length, so you can focus just on the flow direction at each point. Observe that for small f (left), directions vary strongly, while for large f (right), almost every arrow points perfectly horizontally. This underscores how focal length affects directionality as well as speed.

plan.camera_suggestion: 2D orthographic, straight-on, centered to fit one or three image rectangles with full vector field visible and annotated. Zoom out enough so no arrows overlap panel borders even for small f.
plan.notes: Arrow directions and lengths must update reactively on every value change. In comparison mode, render three panels side-by-side (each with independent vector field and their annotated f value beneath) but keep all controls active. For normalized view, arrows in all panels are rescaled to unit length for direction-only analysis. Use clear arrowheads and appropriate grid density (e.g., 12x8 grid per panel) to prevent clutter and overlap at extreme values. All annotations should update live. Prefer sharp, non-rounded panel borders and high-contrast colors for arrows; use a subtle shading or background to visually group each panel.

plan.contextChunk:
\end{bmatrix}
W_Y 
\end{aligned}$$ 

In this case, the focal length $f$ will have a strong
effect on the appearance of the motion field. For very large $f$, we can
approximate the 2D motion field by $\dot{x} \approx f W_Y$ and
$\dot{y} \approx 0$. The resulting motion field is approximately
constant across the entire image and looks like lateral translation
motion. For very small $f$, the motion field will be similar to the one
produced by a homography and it will be very different to a lateral
camera motion. The flows shown in @fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3 correspond
to $f=1/3$, $f=1$, and $f=3$.

![Motion flows corresponding to a rotation around the 
$Y$-axis. (left) $f = 1/3$. (middle) $f = 1$. (right) $f = 3$.](figures/optical_flow/camera_motion_field_rotation_three_f.png){width="100%" #fig-motion_wy_f03_f1_f3}

Camera rotation around the $Y$-axis does not inform about the scene
structure, but it informs about the camera parameters. The magnitude of
$W_Y$ only affects the scaling of the motion vectors, but it does not
change their orientation.

In the case of camera rotation, for a large angle (or a large
$\Delta t$), the relationship between the image at time $t$ and the
image at time $t+\Delta t$ is a homography.

### Motion under Varying Focal Length

Before moving into motion estimation, let's consider one final scenario:
a static camera observing a static scene, but the focal length changes
over time. What will the motion field be? In this setting, despite that

elements: three adjacent rectangular image planes (left, center, right panels)
quiver/vector field of 2D arrows on each panel showing the image motion at grid points
panel annotations indicating focal length: 'f=1/3', 'f=1', and 'f=3' (under each panel)

interactions.label: Focal length f
interactions.teaches: Changing the focal length f transforms the appearance of the motion field: vectors change from a divergent, perspective-like field at small f to nearly uniform translation at large f.

demo_steps.title: Small focal length: divergent field
demo_steps.narration: With a small focal length (f = 1/3), the motion field fans outward from the center. This 'homography-like' pattern mimics the kind of motion you'd see from a perspective projection with strong distortion—motion vectors change direction and magnitude strongly across the image.

demo_steps.title: Intermediate focal length: mixed pattern
demo_steps.narration: At a moderate focal length (f = 1), the arrows still diverge slightly, but the effect is less pronounced. The flow already starts to resemble a sideways translation, although some perspective effect remains.

demo_steps.title: Large focal length: translation field
demo_steps.narration: At large focal length (f = 3), the arrows become nearly uniform and horizontal everywhere: the field is close to pure lateral translation. All motion vectors point in the same direction and have almost constant length, matching the mathematical prediction for rotation with large f.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Orthographic camera perpendicular to the image plane, fitting the entire field of view (single image rectangle) with plenty of margin to see all vectors even as they expand at low f.
interactionPlan.notes: Arrow positions and directions must recompute for every focalLength value, visualized as a vector field over a grid. Arrows should scale in length appropriately for comparison across f, but never overlap or clip outside the panel. Each panel annotation ('f=...') updates dynamically if the focal length slider is used directly for the single interactive panel version.

Drag the slider to change the middle panel.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: Arrows are missing in the generated figure panels.
Panel outlines and labels are present and seem correct.
The interaction for changing focal length is implemented but not visibly impactful without arrows.
evaluation.notes: Arrows are missing, reducing clarity of concept; interactions are present but unclear.
action_items: Add the missing arrows to each panel to represent the velocity fields.
Ensure arrow directions and lengths change with interactions to reflect concept.
Improve the caption or guidance under interactions to clarify expected changes.

feedback.rationale: The Concept-Misunderstood failure mode and missing arrows indicate a fundamental issue with the interaction plan, as the arrows are critical for understanding the concept of velocity fields.
action_items: Add the missing arrows to each panel to represent the velocity fields.
Ensure arrow directions and lengths change with interactions to reflect concept.
Improve the caption or guidance under interactions to clarify expected changes.

action_items: Reduce arrow thickness to match the original image.
